children.name: Latrodectus mactans
children.description: Iconic black widow with red hourglass marking. Venom contains alpha-latrotoxin causing severe muscle cramps; fatalities extremely rare with modern care.

children.name: Latrodectus hasselti
children.description: Australia's most medically significant spider; about 2000 envenomations per year. Identifiable by orange-red dorsal stripe. Antivenom available since 1956.

children.name: Latrodectus tredecimguttatus
children.description: Found around the Mediterranean and Central Asia. Historically associated with the tarantism dance phenomenon in southern Italy.

children.name: Latrodectus geometricus
children.description: Cosmopolitan species with distinctive spiked egg sacs. More potent venom than black widow but injects less and is less aggressive.

children.name: Latrodectus hesperus
children.description: The black widow of western North America, with a shiny black body and red hourglass. Common around buildings and woodpiles; medically significant but bites are rarely fatal.

children.name: Latrodectus variolus
children.description: A black widow of eastern North America distinguished by a row of red spots and broken hourglass on the underside. Builds tangled webs in forests and shrubs.

children.name: Latrodectus bishopi
children.description: A rare widow endemic to Florida scrub habitats, with a reddish-orange head and legs and black abdomen. Restricted to palmetto and threatened by habitat loss.

children.name: Latrodectus katipo
children.description: A threatened widow spider endemic to New Zealand sand dunes, with a red dorsal stripe. Declining due to coastal habitat loss and invasive species; legally protected.

children.name: Latrodectus elegans
children.description: An Asian widow spider of India, China and Japan, glossy black with red markings. Medically significant within its range.

children.name: Latrodectus pallidus
children.description: A pale widow spider of arid North Africa, the Middle East and Central Asia, with a white or cream bulbous abdomen lacking the typical dark hourglass. Its venom is less neurotoxic than that of L. tredecimguttatus.

children.name: Latrodectus corallinus
children.description: A South American widow found in Argentina and Uruguay, with a coral-red body bearing a black hourglass. Its venom is potent and causes latrodectism but fatalities are rare.

children.name: Latrodectus dahli
children.description: A Central Asian widow from Iran, Afghanistan and Turkmenistan, named for the naturalist Friedrich Dahl. The abdomen is black with a red-orange hourglass marking.

children.name: Latrodectus menavodi
children.description: A widow spider endemic to Madagascar, with a glossy black abdomen and extensive red markings dorsally and ventrally. Described by Strand in 1907.

children.name: Latrodectus renivulvatus
children.description: An African widow spider ranging from Sudan to South Africa, distinguished by kidney-shaped genital features. Known for a painful neurotoxic bite but rarely deadly.

children.name: Steatoda grossa
children.description: Most common false widow; regularly mistaken for Latrodectus. Widespread in human structures worldwide including Sweden.

children.name: Steatoda nobilis
children.description: Originally from Macaronesia; now established across western Europe. Britain's most feared spider despite very few documented serious bites.

children.name: Steatoda bipunctata
children.description: A common false widow of European buildings and outhouses, glossy brown with a pale band. Harmless to humans and a frequent house spider.

children.name: Steatoda paykulliana
children.description: A Mediterranean false widow with a black body and red-bordered crescent, sometimes mistaken for a black widow. Its bite is mild compared to true widows.

children.name: Steatoda triangulosa
children.description: A cosmopolitan synanthropic spider with distinctive purplish-brown triangle markings. Common in homes worldwide, where it preys on ants, other spiders and insect pests.

children.name: Steatoda capensis
children.description: A small glossy black false widow native to southern Africa and introduced to New Zealand, where it is a common synanthropic spider sometimes confused with the katipo.

children.name: Steatoda albomaculata
children.description: A Holarctic false widow with a dark brown abdomen bearing white spots. Found in dry, sandy habitats and known to prey on ants.

children.name: Steatoda castanea
children.description: A European false widow with a shining chestnut-brown abdomen and white marginal band. Synanthropic, found in buildings and outhouses across central and eastern Europe.

children.name: Steatoda borealis
children.description: A Holarctic false widow with a purplish-brown abdomen bearing a pale median band. Common in buildings and gardens across northern North America and Europe.

children.name: Steatoda fulva
children.description: A tawny-orange false widow with a dark abdominal pattern, found in the southwestern United States and Mexico.

children.name: Steatoda cingulata
children.description: A banded false widow from the Middle East, Central Asia and North Africa, with a white or pale equatorial band on the abdomen.

children.name: Spintharus davidattenboroughi
children.description: Described from Jamaica in 2018 alongside 14 other new Spintharus species. Named after David Attenborough to honour his advocacy for the natural world and climate action. The genus is distinguished by a smiley-face pattern on the abdomen. Caribbean Spintharus are a remarkable radiation of visually ornamented cobweb spiders whose colour patterns are thought to function in mate recognition.

children.name: Theridion grallator
children.description: A tiny spider endemic to the Hawaiian Islands famous for abdominal markings that resemble a smiling face, varying dramatically between individuals. A favorite of evolutionary studies on color polymorphism.

children.name: Theridion pictum
children.description: A small, colorful cobweb spider with a variegated abdomen, found on low vegetation near water across the Holarctic.

children.name: Theridion varians
children.description: A common Holarctic cobweb spider with a highly variable abdomen that can be yellow, green, white, pink or red with darker markings. Found on shrubs and low trees in old-growth and orchard habitats.

children.name: Theridion melanurum
children.description: A Palearctic cobweb spider with a dark brown abdomen bearing a white median stripe. Synanthropic, found on walls and in buildings.

children.name: Theridion impressum
children.description: A small cobweb spider of Europe and Asia with an abdomen bearing a series of impressed dots (sigilla) forming rows. Builds webs in coniferous and deciduous trees.

children.name: Theridion sisyphium
children.description: A European cobweb spider notable for maternal care: the female feeds her young by regurgitation and shares captured prey, an unusual behaviour among spiders.

children.name: Theridion mystaceum
children.description: A pale European cobweb spider with a distinctive dark moustache-like marking on the carapace. Found under bark and in crevices.

children.name: Anelosimus eximius
children.description: A highly social spider of South American rainforests that lives in colonies of thousands sharing a single giant communal web. Cooperates in prey capture and brood care, an extreme rarity among spiders.

children.name: Anelosimus studiosus
children.description: A widespread American tangle-web spider showing facultative sociality, with some females cooperating in multi-female nests. A model for studying the evolution of social behavior and animal personality.

children.name: Anelosimus elegans
children.description: A Neotropical social spider ranging from Mexico to Brazil, living in large multi-generational communal webs. Prey capture is cooperative.

children.name: Anelosimus ethicus
children.description: The only Asian species of Anelosimus, found from India to Indonesia. Builds tangled communal webs with multiple females cooperating.

children.name: Anelosimus pacificus
children.description: A social cobweb spider from New Caledonia, Vanuatu, the Solomon Islands and Fiji. Colony webs can cover entire shrubs.

children.name: Argyrodes elevatus
children.description: A tiny silvery kleptoparasitic spider that lives in the webs of larger orb-weavers, stealing trapped prey and even eating the host's wrapped meals. The shiny abdomen resembles a dewdrop.

children.name: Argyrodes argentatus
children.description: A silvery kleptoparasitic spider of the Old World tropics, with a teardrop-shaped abdomen that reflects light. Steals prey from orb-weaver webs in forests and gardens.

children.name: Argyrodes flavescens
children.description: A golden-yellow kleptoparasitic spider of Southeast Asia, living in the webs of Nephila and other large orb-weavers. The carapace has a distinctive red eye region.

children.name: Argyrodes cognatus
children.description: An Australasian kleptoparasite that lives in the webs of Trichonephila. Its silver-and-black body mimics a dewdrop hanging in the web.

children.name: Tidarren sisyphoides
children.description: A cobweb spider notable for extreme sexual size dimorphism: the tiny male amputates one of his own palps before maturity and dies during mating, becoming an extreme example of male sacrifice.

children.name: Tidarren haemorrhoidale
children.description: A Neotropical cobweb spider from the southern US to Argentina. The male is tiny compared to the female and amputates one palp before mating.

children.name: Tidarren mirabile
children.description: An African dwarf-male cobweb spider from Madagascar, with extreme sexual dimorphism where the male reaches only 5% of the female's body weight.

children.name: Enoplognatha ovata
children.description: A small Holarctic cobweb spider with a striking variable abdomen that may be plain cream, red-striped or boldly red. Common on low vegetation, where it folds a leaf into a silken retreat.

children.name: Enoplognatha thoracica
children.description: A small Holarctic cobweb spider with a cream abdomen marked by a wavy red or orange dorsal stripe. Found on low vegetation in damp habitats.

children.name: Enoplognatha latimana
children.description: A species formerly confused with E. ovata, distinguished by male palp morphology. The female has a variable red or pink dorsal band. Native to Europe and introduced to North America.

children.name: Enoplognatha mordax
children.description: A Mediterranean and Black Sea candystripe spider with a large, toothed chelicera. Despite the name, not dangerous to humans.

children.name: Chrysso
children.description: A genus of colourful cobweb spiders, often with a distinctive white or yellow tear-drop shaped abdomen marked with black spots and red bands. Many species mimic the colour patterns of venomous wasps or beetles as a defensive strategy.

children.name: Chrysso albomaculata
children.description: An Asian cobweb spider with a white abdomen marked with black and red spots. Found in Japan, China and Taiwan in forest understorey.

children.name: Chrysso spiniventris
children.description: A Southeast Asian Chrysso with a hard, spiny abdomen. The female's abdomen is white with black spots and has a pointed posterior projection.

children.name: Chrysso pulcherrima
children.description: A strikingly patterned Neotropical cobweb spider with a white abdomen bearing black spots and a crimson red band. Ranges from the southern US to Argentina.

children.name: Chrysso vexabilis
children.description: A Hawaiian endemic Chrysso found only on the island of Oahu. The abdomen has a raised, hump-like structure and white-yellow colouration.

children.name: Achaearanea
children.description: A genus of theridiid spiders that build tangled, three-dimensional webs in marshes and wetlands. Recent taxonomic revisions have moved many species to Cryptachaea, Parasteatoda and other genera.

children.name: Achaearanea veruculata
children.description: A spider with a greyish abdomen covered in small wart-like bumps. Native to Asia and Australia; introduced to New Zealand where it is a common house spider.

children.name: Achaearanea trapezoidalis
children.description: An Afro-Asian cobweb spider with a grey abdomen marked by a distinctive trapezoid or diamond pattern. Found in buildings and vegetation.

children.name: Achaearanea wauana
children.description: A cobweb spider from Papua New Guinea, described from the Wau region. Builds typical tangled webs in forest clearings.

children.name: Achaearanea nigrovittata
children.description: An Asian marsh cobweb spider with a grey abdomen marked with dark longitudinal stripes. Found in humid habitats near water across Southeast Asia.

children.name: Cryptachaea
children.description: A genus recently split from Achaearanea, comprising small to medium cobweb spiders with a humped or rounded abdomen. Many species are synanthropic and some have become invasive outside their native range.

children.name: Cryptachaea blattea
children.description: A tiny synanthropic cobweb spider originally from East Asia that has spread globally through horticulture. Often found in greenhouses, where it builds small tangled webs.

children.name: Cryptachaea gigantipes
children.description: A large Achaearanea-like spider from New Zealand and Australia, with notably long, slender legs. Found in natural habitats such as under bark and in leaf litter.

children.name: Cryptachaea veruculata
children.description: An Australasian cobweb spider with a rough, warty abdomen. Introduced to New Zealand where it is a common spider in gardens and buildings.

children.name: Euryopis
children.description: Ant-eating cobweb spiders with a distinctive flattened, oval abdomen that is often brightly marked with cream or white bands and spots. They are specialised ant predators that use rapid, darting attacks.

children.name: Euryopis flavomaculata
children.description: A small ant-eating spider of Europe and Japan, with a dark grey abdomen bearing bright yellow spots. Hunts ants by darting in to bite and retreating.

children.name: Euryopis funebris
children.description: A North American ant-eating spider with a black abdomen marked by two white or cream spots. Found under rocks and debris in arid habitats.

children.name: Euryopis texana
children.description: A Texas ant-eating spider with a flattened, silvery-white abdomen bearing two dark spots. Hunts ants on the ground in open habitats.

children.name: Euryopis quinqueguttata
children.description: A European ant-eating spider with a dark abdomen and five white spots. Found in dry, warm habitats such as limestone grasslands and heathlands.

children.name: Episinus
children.description: A genus of cobweb spiders that build a distinctive H-shaped or ladder-like web, consisting of a few widely spaced guy lines with a single sticky strand at the bottom. The abdomen often bears paired humps or furrows.

children.name: Episinus angulatus
children.description: A small European furrow-bellied spider with two distinct humps on the abdomen. Builds a distinctive ladder-shaped web under leaves in woodland.

children.name: Episinus truncatus
children.description: A Palearctic furrow-bellied spider with a truncated abdomen bearing two rear humps. Found in low vegetation and leaf litter in woodland.

children.name: Episinus maculipes
children.description: A small Episinus with spotted legs and a bi-lobed abdomen. Builds the characteristic reduced web among moss and leaf litter.

children.name: Episinus affinis
children.description: A circum-Mediterranean furrow-bellied spider with a blue-grey abdomen and two prominent tubercles. Found in maquis scrub, under stones and in tree bark.

children.name: Dipoena
children.description: A large genus of small cobweb spiders with a characteristically broad carapace that is wider than the abdomen. They are specialised ant predators like Euryopis, building minimal webs or hunting on the ground.

children.name: Dipoena torva
children.description: A European wide-headed spider found under bark on old trees, especially pines. The carapace is chocolate-brown and much broader than the oval abdomen.

children.name: Dipoena nigra
children.description: A small black cobweb spider with a wide head, living on the ground in moss and leaf litter. Hunts ants by ambush.

children.name: Dipoena prona
children.description: A European wide-headed spider found in treetops and shrubs, where it builds a simple web. The abdomen is brown with a pale median band.

children.name: Dipoena aculeata
children.description: A Holarctic wide-headed spider with a remarkably spiny abdomen. Despite the ferocious appearance, it is a harmless ant predator.

children.name: Dipoena sericata
children.description: A North American wide-headed spider with a silky, reflective abdomen. Found in leaf litter in woodland habitats.

children.name: Thwaitesia
children.description: A genus of small, strikingly beautiful cobweb spiders whose abdomen is covered with silvery or iridescent reflective spots that resemble sequins or mirrors. These reflective patches are thought to confuse predators or attract prey.

children.name: Coleosoma
children.description: A small pantropical genus of cobweb spiders with a glossy, bulbous abdomen that resembles a seed or berry. They build small, inconspicuous tangled webs in vegetation and have been spread globally through the horticultural trade.

children.description: A tiny pantropical spider with a glossy, black pea-shaped abdomen. Originally Neotropical but spread globally in greenhouses. Only 2 mm long.
